dbBikes.motor: 750W (1130W peak) geared rear hub, torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: Aventon's best-selling fat-tire all-rounder. Huge install base means steady takeoff supply: knobby tires, wedge saddles, and Zoom forks come off constantly.

dbBikes.motor: 500W (900W peak) geared rear hub, torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: Torque-sensor commuter workhorse. Ships loaded with rack, fenders, Zoom Aria fork, and Tektro hydros, so takeoffs skew toward comfort parts.
Comfort cruiser with torque sensor and turn signals. Rigid fork and relaxed geometry; takeoffs concentrate in contact points and the BC280 display.
Comfort-first step-thru with a UL-listed 692Wh pack. Mechanical brakes and a flimsy fork (reviewers' words) define the upgrade path and the takeoff stream.
The default halo e-moto. Every owner strips it in month one, so takeoff supply is bottomless and resale is slow.
Sting MX4 and Sting R MX4 are the same bike per GritShift and Talaria dealers, so one entry covers both names. Sur-Ron's main rival with the same strip-it-day-one owner culture.
Sur-Ron derived platform, and many parts physically interchange with the Light Bee X. Same upgrade culture, same flooded takeoff market.
The flagship Super73 with full adjustable suspension. Owners customize hard, but the parts culture is cosmetic and comfort driven rather than performance teardown.

The best selling budget folder in America from 2022 to 2024, replaced by the XP4 in 2025. Owners swap the same four parts in month one: saddle, seatpost, pedals, tires. Takeoff supply is deep and buyer demand from budget repairers is steady.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1310W peak, 85Nm, Stealth M24 on 2.0)
dbBikes.blurb: The budget cargo workhorse, 450 lb payload on the 2.0. Families load it up and upgrade range and rubber first. The rack is part of the frame, so takeoffs skew batteries, tires, and contact points.

Fast 52V commuter from a brand that collapsed into bankruptcy in 2024. Owners cannot buy OEM anything anymore, so working takeoffs, above all batteries, displays, and controllers, carry real scarcity value. Price and hold accordingly.

Trek's entry Bosch commuter and one of the most common premium e-hybrids on the road. Huge parc means steady takeoff flow: Purion displays, 4A chargers, and stock Bontrager contact points come off constantly.
Class 3 flagship commuter with the quiet Brose-based 2.0 motor and a big 710Wh pack. Proprietary electronics mean Specialized takeoffs command replacement-part money from stranded owners.

Dutch belt-drive commuter with the Enviolo stepless hub and Bosch Performance Line. Near zero drivetrain takeoffs since the belt lasts forever, but the Bosch electronics and quality contact points move.
Rad's flagship fat bike, sold in huge numbers from 2021 on. Semi-integrated battery, dual displays, hydraulic brakes. Takeoff supply is deep and parts demand stays steady.
Commuter workhorse with the RadRover 6 Plus electronics in a 27.5 package. Owners swap contact points fast and the battery holds value.
The budget moped-style runabout. Single speed, LED control pad, external battery. Cheap bike, so takeoffs are cheap, except the battery.
Ride1Up's long-running flagship commuter, discontinued in favor of the Vorsa; huge installed base means steady takeoff flow of Selle Royal saddles, Schwalbe tires and Tektro brakes.
46lb budget city bike with a torque sensor. Mechanical discs are the number-one owner complaint, making brake takeoffs the signature Soltera trade-in.
81lb longtail cargo hauler with a 143lb-rated rack and hydraulic dropper post. Takeoffs skew utilitarian; the 720Wh pack is the money part.
Sub-$3k mid-drive eMTB with name-brand parts (RockShox, SRAM, Maxxis). The only Aventon whose takeoffs have a genuine enthusiast resale market.

dbBikes.motor: 750W (1100W peak) geared rear hub, 75Nm, SensorSwap torque/cadence
dbBikes.blurb: Feature-dense commuter: torque sensor, 3.5in color display, hydraulic brakes, full accessory kit. Well-specced stock, so takeoffs concentrate in contact points.
UL-listed fat-tire value bike. CST BFT knobbies come off for street tires constantly, and the shared display and battery SKUs help takeoff liquidity.
36lb stealth urban bike with a frame-integrated non-removable battery. Battery is NOT a takeoff candidate; the trade-in stream is pure contact points and tires.

dbBikes.motor: 12.5kW mid-mounted PMSM with FOC sine controller
dbBikes.blurb: The grown-up Sur-Ron. Better stock parts than the Light Bee but owners still chase Sirris suspension and EBMX power, so takeoffs flow steadily.
Full-size 96V flagship, still in production but thin on the ground in the US. Takeoff parts exist in small numbers and resale evidence is nearly nonexistent.

dbBikes.motor: 6kW peak mid-mounted IPM with belt primary
dbBikes.blurb: Talaria's 17in supermoto pit bike, sold as X3 or xXx. Newer and lower volume than the Sting, so the used parts market is thin and prices must stay careful.
The X260's smaller sibling, big with the teen rider crowd. Parts demand exists but skews cheap, and the buyer pool is smaller than the 60V bikes.
The volume seller of the Super73 lineup. Big install base means steady takeoff flow, but parts are cheap new so used prices stay modest.
Entry-level Super73 with no suspension and a smaller pack. Least teardown culture in the lineup, so takeoff volume and values both run light.
Full-suspension R-series in the Brooklyn trim. Shares the S2/R parts bin, so most takeoffs merge into existing pools.
Cafe-racer moped with real motorcycle speed. Loyal owner base mods steadily but the parts market is a fraction of the Sur-Ron world, so resale is slow and thin.
Lectric's entry folder, refreshed in 2024 with hydraulic brakes and a color display. Single speed keeps it simple. Same comfort-swap pattern as the XP: smaller parts, smaller money.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1310W peak, 85Nm, Stealth M24)
dbBikes.blurb: Torque-sensor commuter at a throwaway price, with an SR Suntour fork and Shimano Altus 8-speed stock. Contact-point swaps dominate; the fork and mech mostly come off when a bike gets parted out.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1310W peak, 85Nm, Stealth M24)
dbBikes.blurb: Lectric's fat tire adventure bike with an RST Renegade fork and hydraulic brakes stock. Riders who use it as intended chew through tires and want more range. Lock-on grips and real suspension mean fewer comfort swaps than the XP line.

dbBikes.motor: 500W mid-mounted jackshaft motor driving a rear differential (1092W peak, 65Nm)
dbBikes.blurb: The default budget electric trike until the Trike2 replaced it in 2025. Buyers skew older and want backrest saddles almost on day one. Hydraulic brakes, single speed, and the XP-pattern battery keep parts interchangeable and moving.

Juiced's best seller: a 28 mph fat tire commuter with a Shimano 9-speed and hydraulic brakes. The brand's 2024 collapse turned every working takeoff into supported-life insurance for stranded owners. Batteries and controllers are the money parts.

Full-suspension 52V moped, orphaned when Juiced folded in 2024. Almost nothing on it cross-shops with standard bike parts, which cuts both ways: takeoffs are scarce and wanted, but the only buyers are other Scorpion owners.

dbBikes.motor: Bosch Performance Line 65Nm mid-drive (Gen 2: Performance Line CX 85Nm)
dbBikes.blurb: Trek's serious commuter with the integrated PowerTube and full rack, fender, and light kit. Owners upgrade contact points fast and part-outs feed a real PowerTube and Purion takeoff market.
Upright comfort cruiser on the same MasterMind platform as the Vado. Comfort buyers swap saddles and grips immediately, and the 530 to 710 battery upgrade is a known path.
Gazelle's entry Bosch commuter with a standard Shimano 9-speed drivetrain. Ships with the slow 2A compact charger, which makes the charger upgrade the most predictable sale on this bike.
Giant's Yamaha-powered trekking commuter. Proprietary Giant electronics mean thinner takeoff demand than Bosch parts, but batteries and chargers still find captive replacement buyers.
Cannondale's step-thru Bosch commuter, the Verve+ 2's direct rival. Same Purion and 2A charger story: predictable takeoffs and a guaranteed charger upsell.
2023 refresh of the RadRunner with hydraulic brakes and a suspension fork. Newer proprietary electronics mean a thin used market for now.
Gen 1 RadRunner with the works: LCD, suspension fork, fenders, passenger-ready. Discontinued, so its parts feed the big RadRunner fleet still on the road.
The 22-inch-wheel cargo hauler with the infamous tire recall. Never resell the stock tires. Everything else trades like standard Rad kit.
2024 cargo redo: torque sensor, 720Wh Safe Shield pack, hydraulic brakes, standard 20-inch wheels. Too new for a real takeoff market yet.
Folding 20x4 runabout on the classic Rad external-battery platform. Same electronics as the old fleet, so parts cross over.
Discontinued single-speed city bike on a lighter 500W platform. Batteries are the only takeoff with real legs; owners hunt spares.
The classic external-battery fat bike that built Rad. Biggest mod community of the bunch; Bolton kits, tire swaps and hydraulic conversions are routine.
The $1,095 entry commuter that reviewers keep calling the value pick; owners upgrade the Tourney-class shifting and coil fork first, and the no-name takeoffs are worth little.
The cheapest real Brose mid-drive in the US market; parts spec is a tier above the rest of this list, so its takeoffs (Tektro HD-M745s, microShift Advent, Maxxis) actually hold some value.
33 lb stealth belt-drive single-speed, replaced by the V3; minimalist build means few takeoffs, and the battery is sealed in the frame so it never trades.
The torque-sensor commuter that rides a tier above its price; V1 shipped an 80mm air fork, V2 a Suntour XCM32, and its Samsung-cell Eel battery is the best takeoff on the bike.
Best-selling budget folding fat bike; everything on it is no-name except the Shimano 7-speed, so takeoffs are near worthless but the saddle, tires and seatpost churn constantly.
UL-listed 28 mph step-thru folder sold through Best Buy; rare 750W folding format, but the component takeoffs are the same near-worthless budget fare as the Mars.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1000W peak; 2.0 up to 1200W peak)
dbBikes.blurb: 83 lb full-suspension budget folder that reviewers call a great canvas for customization; the big 16Ah battery and the controller are the only takeoffs with a pulse.
The $1,050 bestseller of budget folding fat bikes; entry-level everything, and the mechanical disc brakes are the one takeoff nearly every owner replaces.

dbCatalog.name: Stock 48V 10-14Ah battery pack
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The range-anxiety cure: bigger 52V pack

dbCatalog.name: Stock 500-750W hub motor (wheel included)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Hub-to-mid-drive is the big-league swap

dbCatalog.name: Stock controller
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: More amps, smoother throttle mapping

dbCatalog.name: Stock tire pair (street or fat)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Street rubber transforms fat-tire commuters

dbCatalog.name: Stock mechanical disc brake set
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The #1 upgrade on every cable-brake bike

dbCatalog.name: Stock hydraulic brake set (MT200 class)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: MT200s work; MT5e's stop a loaded bike

dbCatalog.name: Stock LCD display
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: App-programmable and half the size

dbCatalog.name: Zoom 80mm hydraulic suspension fork (Aventure)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The stock Zoom is a pogo stick with a lockout. The USD-8 actually damps instead of just bouncing.
dbCatalog.comp_note: eBay Zoom fork listings run $24-95 used but none are confirmed Aventure-spec solds. Priced low; unbranded OEM forks move slowly.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 720Wh battery (Aventure.2)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Proprietary mount, so a second OEM pack is the only real play. Double your range, keep the warranty.
dbCatalog.comp_note: New from Aventon ~$550; generic 48V 15Ah packs sell new on eBay for $170-265, which caps what buyers pay for a used proprietary pack. One eBay sold confirmed but price hidden. Priced low.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon BC280 color display
dbCatalog.comp_note: New retail $69.99 direct from Aventon. Two eBay used listings found (Pace 500 and Sinch 2 fitment) but no confirmed sold prices. Buyers are owners with cracked screens.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 26x4 stock knobby tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Knobbies hum like a truck on pavement. Speedsters cut the noise and add real range.
dbCatalog.comp_note: New Kenda 26x4 equivalents (Juggernaut, Krusade) list $30-55 per tire on eBay, so a used unbranded takeoff pair tops out around $35-45. Street-tire conversions make this the most common Aventure takeoff.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon stock wedge saddle
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The stock wedge punishes anyone over an hour in the seat. The Cloud-9 is the default fix on r/aventon.
dbCatalog.comp_note: No confirmed solds; generic OEM saddles sell $10-20 on eBay. First thing most owners swap per forum threads.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon stock ergonomic lock-on grips
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Numb hands on long rides come from round grips. GP1 wings fix it for forty bucks.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Near-zero resale demand for stock rubber grips. Priced at parts-bin level.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon stock alloy platform pedals
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Stock pedal bearings die young. Chesters grip wet shoes and shrug off rock strikes.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Owner reports of failing bearings in stock pedals. Used OEM platforms sell $8-15 when they sell at all.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Aventure/Abound)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: A 77lb bike at 28mph deserves 4-piston calipers and thicker rotors. Stock 2-pistons fade on long descents.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Forum complaints about rotor harmonics and thin 1.8mm stock rotors drive upgrades. No confirmed used-set solds; new e-bike hydraulic sets run $85-160, capping the takeoff.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon rear rack (Aventure)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Comes stock so takeoff supply exists, but demand is thin since every bike ships with one. Priced accordingly.

dbCatalog.name: Zoom Aria suspension fork (Level.2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Zoom forks list $24-95 used on eBay with no confirmed Aria solds. Commuters rarely swap it, so supply and demand are both thin.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 672Wh battery (Level)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Commuters kill packs with daily charging. A second OEM battery doubles range and halves cycle wear.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Facebook Marketplace listing referenced $450 new retail for the Level pack. Generic 48V packs at $170-265 new cap the used ceiling. Priced low.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 27.5x2.1 stock tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: A commuter with a flat is a paperweight. Marathon Plus is the boring correct answer.
dbCatalog.comp_note: No resale datapoints found for the OEM tire specifically. Unbranded takeoff commuter pairs move at $20-30. Priced low.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon rigid alloy seatpost
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: No rear suspension means your spine is the shock. The NCX does the job for under a hundred bucks.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Suspension seatpost is THE Level.2 comfort mod, so rigid takeoff posts pile up. Almost no buyers; priced at floor.

dbCatalog.name: Tektro hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Level.2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: EBR confirms Tektro hydros stock. New HD-E350 sets run $85-160, so used takeoff sets land near $45. Takeoffs mostly come from crash repairs.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 614Wh battery (Pace 500)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Only OEM packs fit the frame slot. Second battery beats range anxiety.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Smaller pack than Aventure/Level so priced below them. No confirmed solds; generic new packs cap the ceiling. Priced low.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon Pace cruiser saddle
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The stock seat is decent for a cruiser. Riders past 15 miles a day still want the big spring.
dbCatalog.comp_note: EBR confirms the Pace gets a thicker rounded seat, not the wedge. Slightly better than wedge resale but still a saddle.

dbCatalog.name: Bafang 750W rear hub motor (27.5, Juiced)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Bafang hub motor wheels list $120-250 on eBay new and used; laced takeoffs priced at the low end pending sold data.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 27.5x2.2 stock tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Flat protection is the only tire upgrade a cruiser needs.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Street tread already, so fewer swaps than the fat bikes. No solds found; priced with the Level pair.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon mechanical disc brake set (Soltera.2)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Cable discs on a 28mph-capable bike is a cost cut you fix first. Pre-bled set bolts right on.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Mech-to-hydraulic conversion is the defining Soltera.2 upgrade, so takeoff supply is high and demand near zero. Floor pricing.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 346Wh battery (Soltera)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The 346Wh pack is the bike's weak spot. A spare in the pannier doubles a thin range.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Small 36V pack worth well under the 48V units. No confirmed solds; priced low against generic 36V packs at $120-180 new.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon LCD display (Soltera.2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Could not confirm whether Soltera.2 shares the BC280; kept as its own entry to avoid a bad merge. Priced under the BC280.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 700x38c stock tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Commuters flat on glass and grates. Marathons end that conversation.
dbCatalog.comp_note: No solds found for the OEM tire. Generic 700c takeoff pairs move at $15-25.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 720Wh battery (Abound)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Cargo miles with kids aboard eat watt-hours. Second pack or shorter school runs, pick one.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Same 720Wh capacity as Aventure but a model-specific SKU, so kept separate from the Aventure pack. Priced to match; no confirmed solds.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 50mm cargo suspension fork (Abound)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Cargo-specific 20-inch fork with almost no cross-compatibility. Thin market both directions.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 20x2.4 cargo tires (pair)
dbCatalog.comp_note: No resale market found for stock 20-inch cargo takeoff tires. Nobody is buying these used.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon hydraulic dropper post (Abound)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Droppers have a real used market but this is an OEM unit with unknown cross-fit. Priced conservatively.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon cargo saddle (Abound)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Hauling kids means stop-and-go riding all day. Wide and sprung wins.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Standard saddle economics: high supply, low demand.

dbCatalog.name: RockShox 35 Silver TK 130mm fork
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The 35 Silver is a coil fork doing its best. A Pike transforms the front end on real trails.
dbCatalog.comp_note: eBay listings found at ~C$261 (120mm, used) and ~C$310 (160mm, new takeoff), roughly US$190-225 asks. Sold medians run below asks; priced at $150. Real market exists but under 3 confirmed solds.

dbCatalog.name: SRAM NX Eagle 12-speed drivetrain takeoff
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: NX shifts fine until it is muddy. GX is the cheapest real improvement mid-drive torque deserves.
dbCatalog.comp_note: eBay NX Eagle groupsets list $125-559 new depending on completeness; a used 4-piece takeoff realistically clears ~$140. High-volume market but no confirmed sold prices in results.

dbCatalog.name: SRAM DB8 4-piston brake set
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: DB8s stop fine. Codes stop fine all the way down the mountain, every lap.
dbCatalog.comp_note: DB8s retail ~$95 per wheel new. Used takeoff sets should clear $80. Already 4-piston, so takeoffs mostly come from lever-feel preference swaps.

dbCatalog.name: Maxxis Rekon 29x2.4 tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Rekons roll fast and wash out in corners. Minions bite. Standard eMTB swap.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Used takeoff Rekons routinely move at $25-35 per tire in classifieds. Priced a pair at $50; no confirmed sold triplet.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 708Wh battery (Ramblas)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Turbo mode on trails drains it in 30 miles. Spare pack in the truck doubles the ride day.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Newest and priciest Aventon pack but zero used-market history yet. Priced with the 48V packs to stay conservative.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon MTB saddle (Ramblas)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Saddles are personal but the Volt is the safest default in mountain biking.
dbCatalog.comp_note: OEM saddle, personal-fit item, minimal resale.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon lock-on MTB grips (Ramblas)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Thin OEM rubber beats your palms on descents. Proper MTB grips are cheap insurance.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Parts-bin value.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon Ramblas stock platform pedals
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Real pins, rebuildable, cheap. No reason to ride the stockers.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Throw-in pedals on an otherwise name-brand build. First swap for most buyers.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric mechanical disc brake set (Discover 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The single most requested Discover 1 fix. Pre-bled kit, one afternoon, done.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Mech discs on a 60lb ebike drive the hydraulic conversion, so takeoffs are plentiful and nearly unsellable. Floor pricing.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 692Wh battery (Discover 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Proprietary tray, so OEM spare is the only option. All battery sales final per Velotric.
dbCatalog.comp_note: UL cert helps resale but no confirmed solds surfaced. Velotric sells spares new (price not confirmed in results); priced low against the generic-pack ceiling.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric color LCD display (Discover 1 / Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Velotric sells replacements direct, which caps the used price. Buyers are crash-damage owners. No solds found.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock suspension fork (Discover 1)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Reviewers call it flimsy but there is no standard aftermarket swap for this fitment. Thin takeoff market.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 26x2.5 gumwall tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Unbranded gumwalls flat early and often. Big Bens keep the cruiser look and add puncture belts.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Unbranded gumwalls have essentially no resale demand. Priced at floor.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock comfort saddle
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Stock seat is decent. Bigger riders still land here.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Wide comfort saddle stock, so swap rate is lower than Aventon but still steady. Minimal resale.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock ergonomic lock-on grips
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Upright bars load your palms. GP1s spread the load.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Parts-bin value.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock alloy platform pedals
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Same story as every OEM pedal: fine until the bearings quit.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Generic OEM platforms.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric rear rack (Discover)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Ships on every Discover, so used demand is weak.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 706Wh battery (Discover 2 / Summit 1 / Nomad 2)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Cross-compatible across three Velotric models. Best battery investment in their lineup.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Velotric confirms this SKU fits Discover 2, Summit 1, and Nomad 2, which widens the buyer pool. Still no used solds; priced low.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 3.5in color display (Discover 2 / T1 / Nomad 2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Velotric's replacement guide covers D2/S1/T1 ST Plus/N2/B1 with one display SKU, so takeoffs have a multi-model buyer pool. Factory replacements cap the price. No solds found.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock suspension fork (Discover 2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Adequate stock fork, rare swap, thin market.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 27.5 commuter tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Daily commuters buy flat protection once and stop thinking about it.
dbCatalog.comp_note: No OEM-specific solds found. Priced with other unbranded commuter pairs.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Discover 2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Already hydraulic from the factory, so takeoffs come from crash repair, not upgrades. Thin supply, thin demand.

dbCatalog.name: CST BFT 26x4 fat tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: If the Nomad never leaves pavement, the knobbies are just noise and drag. Speedsters fix both.
dbCatalog.comp_note: CST BFTs are a real branded tire that sells new around $40-55 each, so takeoff pairs clear ~$40. Street conversion is the top Nomad mod. No confirmed sold triplet.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 691Wh battery (Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Fat tires eat charge. Spare pack or shorter rides.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Samsung/LG 21700 cells and UL cert support resale, but no confirmed solds surfaced. Priced low with the Discover 1 pack.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 80mm hydraulic fat fork (Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Same swap as every fat hub-drive: air spring and real damping over the stock boinger.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Unbranded OEM fat fork; upgraders pull it but few buyers want it. Priced low.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: 73 pounds of bike plus rider at 28mph. Brake like you mean it.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Unbranded hydros have weak resale. Takeoffs come from riders moving to 4-piston setups.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric sport saddle (T1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Fitness posture wants a performance saddle, not a couch. Volt is the safe pick.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Sport saddle, personal-fit item, minimal resale.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric slick commuter tires (T1, pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: A 36lb commuter with flat protection is the whole point of the T1.
dbCatalog.comp_note: OEM slicks with no aftermarket following. Exact stock size unverified in sources, so priced at floor.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric hydraulic disc brake set (T1)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Hydraulic from the factory; takeoffs are crash-repair driven only.

dbCatalog.name: DNM RCP-2S 10.5in rear shock (Sur-Ron/Segway takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: First thing everyone bins. The stock DNM is the fuse in the system, this one actually damps.
dbCatalog.comp_note: New RCP-2S retails $165 at Luna Cycle. Takeoff pulls saturate r/Surron and eBay and sit unsold; no confirmed sold prices visible, so pegged well under half of new.

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron Light Bee stock inverted fork (DNM takeoff)
